In Figure f:apparent_gamma the 15 GHz speed for 1803+784 is shown with a cross; and the 43 GHz _ app value, with the 15 GHz luminosity, is shown with the letter J. It is likely that we have reported a component speed that is not indicative of the beam speed for 1803+784 . In Figure 8, source 1803+784 is in a cluster of objects that, formally, have negative speed. However, they all are within 1 of zero, and their negativity is of little significance.

sources.summary: The paper models 1803+784 as a BL Lac object whose polarized radio core, one-sided jet, and on-axis polarization peaks are consistent with recollimation shocks.

quotes.quote: The extragalactic radio source 1803+784 is a BL Lac object that shows rapid variability and strong linear polarization. Very long baseline interferometry observations reveal that the core possesses a distinctive structure in polarized intensity with two maxima on axis and two minima symmetrically placed on either side. The approximately radial pattern of electric field polarization rods is reminiscent of the results obtained earlier by Cawthorne (2006) for conical shocks, but, individually, these do not reproduce the main features of the polarized intensity images.

The compact, flat-spectrum radio source 1803+784 is associated with a BL Lac object at redshift z=0.6797 (Lawrence et al., 1996). The structure of the radio source takes the form of a one-sided and apparently curved jet.

The similarity between the observed and model polarization supports the view that the core structure in 1803+784 represents a recollimation shock, and that such shock waves may be responsible for the first disturbance and hence brightening of the quiescent flow in astrophysical jets.

sources.summary: The paper monitors the BL Lac S5 1803+784, linking optical/gamma-ray variability with VLBI jet components and describing its LSP spectral energy distribution.

quotes.quote: This paper is an observational contribution to the study of S5 1803+784, a radio-selected BL Lac object 1981ApJ...247L..53B at z=0.683 1996ApJS..107..541L ; a detailed modeling of the emission processes and morphology is beyond the scope of this paper. S5 1803+784 is characterized by large variations in the optical range 2002AJ....124...53N, 2012AcPol..52a..39N and is well detected at ray energies by the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) 2009ApJ...697.1071A , from the first LAT Catalog of AGN 2009ApJ...700..597A , up to the most recent one 2020ApJ...892..105A . In the taxonomical classification scheme of BL Lac objects by 1995ApJ...444..567P the source is Low Synchrotron Peaked (LSP), with the synchrotron emission peaked around 10 ^ 13 Hz: this is clearly shown by its broadband SED 2002AJ....124...53N .

The morphology of the source, traced at mas resolution by the VLBI radio observations covering a span of several years after the large ray-optical flares, shows a clearly detected component moving outwards after the first flare, slowly decreasing in flux. Another similar component appears to have been generated by the second flare, although with a smaller apparent velocity in the plane of the sky. This finding strongly supports a causal connection between the mechanisms producing the high-energy and the radio-band radiation in blazars.

sources.summary: The paper reports transverse Faraday Rotation gradients across the jet of 1803+784 at multiple epochs, interpreting them as evidence for a helical magnetic field and noting a later gradient reversal.

quotes.quote: However, we find surprising new evidence for a reversal in the direction of the Faraday Rotation gradient across the jet of 1803+784, for which multi-wavelength polarization observations are available at four epochs. At all four epochs, we observe transverse Rotation Measure (RM) gradients across the jet, consistent with the presence of a helical magnetic field wrapped around the jet. However, we also observe a ``flip'' in the direction of the gradient between June 2000 and August 2002.

Transverse RM gradients are visible across the VLBI jet of 1803+784 at all three of our epochs, on scales of 2-3 mas (Figs. 1-3); we are tentatively able to follow it even further from the core. This strongly supports the hypothesis that this jet has a helical magnetic field, consistent with the observed transverse magnetic-field structure (Fig. 5).

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes VLBI monitoring of S5 1803+784, finding mostly stationary inner jet features with changing position angles and a likely geometric origin.

quotes.quote: We present a kinematic analysis of jet component motion in the pc-scale jet of the BL Lac object S5 1803+784, which does not reveal long-term outward motion for most of the components. S5 1803+784 shows complex kinematic phenomena; understanding these provides new insights into the emission processes in BL Lac objects and possibly into the differences between quasars and BL Lac objects. The blazar S5 1803+784 has been studied with VLBI at =1.6, 2.3, 5, 8.4, and 15 GHz between 1993.88 and 2005.68 in 26 observing runs.

The source shows a pronounced jet with prominent jet components located at core separations of = 1.4 , 5, and 12 mas (e.g., Eckart et al. 1986). Geodetic and astronomical VLBI data gathered at 8.4 and 5 GHz between 1979--1987 showed that the component located at =1.4  mas appears stationary (e.g., Schalinski et al. 1988, Witzel et al. 1988).

gmw_00001003.name: LEDA 2191078
gmw_00001003.overview: LEDA 2191078 is a blue-cloud galaxy located in the Perseus cluster that is likely a late major merger [arXiv:2006.07631, arXiv:2112.08728]. It exhibits a non-centric double-peaked inner structure [arXiv:2006.07631] and a one-sided radio tail caused by ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:2112.08728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 2191078 as one of four Perseus jellyfish galaxies with a LOFAR radio tail, ram-pressure morphology, steep tail spectra, and leading-side star formation.

quotes.quote: We identified four jellyfish galaxies (LEDA 2191078, MCG +07-07-070, UGC 2654, UGC 2665) within the central 2^ 2^ ( 2.6 Mpc 2.6 Mpc ) of Perseus based on the presence of one-sided radio continuum tails that were detected at 144 MHz by the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR). The observed radio tails, as well as the orientation of morphological features in the rest-frame optical, are consistent with these four galaxies being impacted by ram pressure stripping as they orbit through the Perseus intracluster medium.

We show that the spectral indices over the galaxy disks are quite flat, while the indices of the stripped tails are substantially steeper. We also identified a number of compact H + [ N ii ] sources with narrowband imaging from the Isaac Newton Telescope. These sources are brighter along the leading side of the galaxy (i.e., \ opposite to the direction of the stripped tail), which is consistent with ram pressure induced star formation.

sources.summary: The paper describes J031633.9+420133 as a likely late major merger with a non-centric double-peaked inner structure, lopsided outskirts, strong H emission, Balmer absorption, and blue-cloud colours.

quotes.quote: (2) \ J031633.9+420133 ( 379) This is probably another late major merger. The inner structure is non-centric and complex with two brightness peaks at a separation of about 4 , the outer part appears slightly lopsided. With its strong H emission and substantial Balmer absorption (EW(H ) = -4 ) this galaxy resembles the type of E+A (post-starburst) galaxies. The SDSS colours place it into the blue cloud.

gmw_00001004.name: MCG +07-07-070
gmw_00001004.overview: MCG +07-07-070 is a star-forming perturbed galaxy located in the Perseus cluster [arXiv:2405.13501, arXiv:2112.08728]. It is undergoing ram-pressure stripping, which has created filamentary dusty and stellar structures [arXiv:2405.13501] alongside a cometary radio tail [arXiv:2405.13501, arXiv:2112.08728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MCG +07-07-070 as a star-forming perturbed jellyfish galaxy in Perseus, with filaments and radio tails indicating a ram-pressure-stripping event.

quotes.quote: Euclid view of known jellyfish galaxies in the Perseus cluster Two star-forming perturbed galaxies, UGC 2665 (49:51:50.7, 41:38:07) and MCG +07-07-070 (50:05:30.9, 41:38:27) are included within the ERO images of the Perseus cluster. These objects are characterised by filamentary dusty and stellar structures escaping from their stellar discs, like those observed in a few cluster objects with available HST data 2015AJ....150...59K,2016AJ....152...32A,2019ApJ...870...63C . The two galaxies also have extended radio continuum tails of cometary shape 2022ApJ...941...77R . All this observational evidence suggests that the two galaxies are undergoing a ram-pressure-stripping event 2022A&ARv..30....3B .

We use the optical and NIR imaging data of extraordinary quality gathered during the Euclid ERO programme of the Perseus cluster to study the impact of the perturbing process on the galaxy properties down to scales of 50 - 100 pc. The data are first used to identify the dominant perturbing mechanism, hydrodynamical versus gravitational, since the two processes have different effects on the stellar component at very low surface brightness levels, such as those reached by . We then compare the observed colours of the filamentary structures formed during the interaction to date their epoch of formation with the purpose of posing strong observational constraints on models and simulations.

sources.summary: The paper identifies MCG +07-07-070 as one of four Perseus jellyfish galaxies with a LOFAR radio tail, ram-pressure morphology, steep tail spectra, and leading-side star formation.

quotes.quote: We identified four jellyfish galaxies (LEDA 2191078, MCG +07-07-070, UGC 2654, UGC 2665) within the central 2^ 2^ ( 2.6 Mpc 2.6 Mpc ) of Perseus based on the presence of one-sided radio continuum tails that were detected at 144 MHz by the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR). The observed radio tails, as well as the orientation of morphological features in the rest-frame optical, are consistent with these four galaxies being impacted by ram pressure stripping as they orbit through the Perseus intracluster medium.

We show that the spectral indices over the galaxy disks are quite flat, while the indices of the stripped tails are substantially steeper. We also identified a number of compact H + [ N ii ] sources with narrowband imaging from the Isaac Newton Telescope. These sources are brighter along the leading side of the galaxy (i.e., \ opposite to the direction of the stripped tail), which is consistent with ram pressure induced star formation.

gmw_00001005.name: UGC 2654
gmw_00001005.overview: UGC 2654 is a jellyfish galaxy located within the central region of the Perseus cluster [arXiv:2112.08728]. It exhibits a one-sided radio continuum tail and leading-side star formation, which are consistent with the effects of ram pressure stripping [arXiv:2112.08728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 2654 as one of four Perseus jellyfish galaxies with a LOFAR radio tail, ram-pressure morphology, steep tail spectra, and leading-side star formation.

quotes.quote: We identified four jellyfish galaxies (LEDA 2191078, MCG +07-07-070, UGC 2654, UGC 2665) within the central 2^ 2^ ( 2.6 Mpc 2.6 Mpc ) of Perseus based on the presence of one-sided radio continuum tails that were detected at 144 MHz by the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR). The observed radio tails, as well as the orientation of morphological features in the rest-frame optical, are consistent with these four galaxies being impacted by ram pressure stripping as they orbit through the Perseus intracluster medium.

We show that the spectral indices over the galaxy disks are quite flat, while the indices of the stripped tails are substantially steeper. We also identified a number of compact H + [ N ii ] sources with narrowband imaging from the Isaac Newton Telescope. These sources are brighter along the leading side of the galaxy (i.e., \ opposite to the direction of the stripped tail), which is consistent with ram pressure induced star formation.

gmw_00001006.name: UGC 2665
gmw_00001006.overview: UGC 2665 is a giant spiral disk galaxy [arXiv:0709.1336, arXiv:2006.07631] located in the Perseus cluster [arXiv:2405.13501]. It is undergoing ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:2405.13501, arXiv:2112.08728], which has created filamentary dusty and stellar structures [arXiv:2405.13501], an extended radio tail [arXiv:2405.13501, arXiv:2112.08728], and active star formation [arXiv:2405.13501, arXiv:2112.08728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 2665 as a star-forming perturbed jellyfish galaxy in Perseus, with dusty/stellar filaments, an extended radio tail, and evidence for ongoing ram-pressure stripping.

quotes.quote: Euclid view of known jellyfish galaxies in the Perseus cluster Two star-forming perturbed galaxies, UGC 2665 (49:51:50.7, 41:38:07) and MCG +07-07-070 (50:05:30.9, 41:38:27) are included within the ERO images of the Perseus cluster. These objects are characterised by filamentary dusty and stellar structures escaping from their stellar discs, like those observed in a few cluster objects with available HST data 2015AJ....150...59K,2016AJ....152...32A,2019ApJ...870...63C . The two galaxies also have extended radio continuum tails of cometary shape 2022ApJ...941...77R . All this observational evidence suggests that the two galaxies are undergoing a ram-pressure-stripping event 2022A&ARv..30....3B .

We use the optical and NIR imaging data of extraordinary quality gathered during the Euclid ERO programme of the Perseus cluster to study the impact of the perturbing process on the galaxy properties down to scales of 50 - 100 pc. The data are first used to identify the dominant perturbing mechanism, hydrodynamical versus gravitational, since the two processes have different effects on the stellar component at very low surface brightness levels, such as those reached by . We then compare the observed colours of the filamentary structures formed during the interaction to date their epoch of formation with the purpose of posing strong observational constraints on models and simulations.

sources.summary: The paper describes J031927.4+413807 as a giant disc galaxy with asymmetric spiral structure, a possible tidal interaction, a candidate tail, wAGN spectrum, H detection, and green-valley colours.

quotes.quote: (3) \ J031927.4+413807 ( 656, UGC 2665) This is a giant disc galaxy with an irregular, asymmetric spiral pattern starting from a blue inner ring at a galactocentric distance of about 3 kpc. The eastern part of the disc, outside the ring, shows some irregular structure (Fig. fig:App_656 , left), which might have been caused by the interaction with the smaller galaxy that is seen to the south of J031927.4+413807 (Fig. fig:App_656 , right). The latter shows an extended low-surface brightness potentially tidal tail that points directly towards the distorted region. J031927.4+413807 has a star-like nucleus and is spectroscopically classified as a wAGN. It is detected as a H source, its SDSS magnitudes are consistent with a green valley galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 2665 as one of four Perseus jellyfish galaxies with a LOFAR radio tail, ram-pressure morphology, steep tail spectra, and leading-side star formation.

quotes.quote: We identified four jellyfish galaxies (LEDA 2191078, MCG +07-07-070, UGC 2654, UGC 2665) within the central 2^ 2^ ( 2.6 Mpc 2.6 Mpc ) of Perseus based on the presence of one-sided radio continuum tails that were detected at 144 MHz by the LOw Frequency ARray (LOFAR). The observed radio tails, as well as the orientation of morphological features in the rest-frame optical, are consistent with these four galaxies being impacted by ram pressure stripping as they orbit through the Perseus intracluster medium.

We show that the spectral indices over the galaxy disks are quite flat, while the indices of the stripped tails are substantially steeper. We also identified a number of compact H + [ N ii ] sources with narrowband imaging from the Isaac Newton Telescope. These sources are brighter along the leading side of the galaxy (i.e., \ opposite to the direction of the stripped tail), which is consistent with ram pressure induced star formation.

sources.summary: The paper reports that UGC 2665 was not detected as one of the early-type nuclear X-ray sources and identifies it as a likely outlying spiral galaxy rather than a Perseus-core early type.

quotes.quote: We do not detect a three-armed spiral galaxy (UGC 2665, listed in Brunzendorf Meusinger 1999), which lies between sources 7 and 9 (Fig. fig:picture4 ). This morphology excludes it being an early-type galaxy. Moreover, the velocity of this galaxy is 2800 higher than for the Perseus cluster core, so it is likely an outlier.

gmw_00001007.name: UGC 7321
gmw_00001007.overview: UGC 7321 is an isolated, bulgeless, low-surface-brightness edge-on galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0307315, arXiv:2208.13527] characterized by a superthin stellar disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0405142, arXiv:2208.13527] and a massive, dark-matter-dominated halo [arXiv:1007.4693, arXiv:2004.05627]. The galaxy exhibits vertical flaring of its gas and stellar disks [arXiv:1003.3110, arXiv:1905.02735], along with extraplanar dust emission [arXiv:2207.11589] and extended H I outskirts [arXiv:1003.3110, arXiv:1303.2463].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC7321 as an Sd galaxy with extended high-latitude HI, visible vertical flaring, figure-eight kinematics, and supporting evidence for a dynamical bar.

quotes.quote: UGC7321 This Sd galaxy extends to a maximum radial extent of 288 \ or 14.0 kpc (assuming the distance of 10 kpc adopted by um2003 ). The scalelength of the projected distribution, h_X , is 112 \ or 5.4 kpc, with the edge of the \ disk at 2.6h_X . This galaxy exhibits substantial high latitude \ extending up to 144 \ or 7.0 kpc in the inner \ disk.

However despite the relatively large synthesised beam, the vertical flaring is clearly visible in most channel maps due to the extended roughly flat rotation curve which is clearly apparent in the XV map (Fig. fig:ch2-ugc7321-combi-plot ).

The XV diagram (Fig. fig:ch2-ugc7321-combi-plot ) also exhibits the characteristic ``figure-8'' shape, which can be indicative of barred \ dynamics km1995,bf1999,ab1999,athanassoula2000 . Further evidence for a dynamical bar in UGC7321 was later found by Pohlen who detected the distinctive boxy-bulge shape in the near-IR distribution pbld2003 . pbld2003 measure the bar length as 112 21 \ or 5.4 1.0 kpc. This is in agreement with the bar size inferred from the size of the ``figure-8'' kinematics. It also corresponds to the scalelength of the projected \ profile.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 as a thin, bulgeless, low-surface-brightness galaxy with a massive dark halo and rules out a proposed bar in its model.

quotes.quote: UGC 7321 Thin stellar disc of UGC 7321 and a lack of bulge indicates that the galaxy owns a massive dark halo. The notation in the left panel is kept the same as for UGC 6080. In the right panel we show the model thickness for =3.4 (dotted curve) and =1.8 (solid curve). The best-fitting model with =3.4 has M_d=0.53 10^ 10 M_ and M_h=1.8 10^ 10 M_ .

The stellar disc contributes 60 into the circular velocity at =2.2L . The central surface density in this case is only _0=220 M_ / pc ^2 . This highlights the low surface brightness nature of the disc inferred by . We investigate a case of the bar formation in this galaxy, according to the hypothesis by and make the conclusion that it cannot exist in UGC 7321, according to our modelling.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7321 as a well-known low surface brightness galaxy, the best resolved sample member, with a symmetric H I map, outskirts warping, possible gas halo, and asymmetric PV diagram.

quotes.quote: UGC 7321 is a well known low surface brightness galaxy, which has already been studied using the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope (WSRT) Garcia-Ruiz2002A and the VLA Uson2003A . The observations by Uson2003A form the basis of our work here. In terms of noise levels, it is the best resolved galaxy in our sample.

The zeroth moment map fig:UGC7321-doublemoment0 shows a well behaved, symmetric galaxy. Only at the outskirts is there some indication of warping, a feature already noted by Matthews2003A , who also notes is flaring. Additionally, they find evidence that suggests the presence of a neutral gas halo. We believe that this halo might be visible as the extended structure on the lower figure, between 0 and 110 arcseconds. The PV-diagram of the galaxy is asymmetric.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 with high signal-to-noise data, finding a steeply rising rotation curve, measured velocity dispersion, and 24 percent hidden H I.

quotes.quote: The galaxy is the only in our sample which has been observed using the VLA. Together with ESO 274-G001, the galaxy has the highest signal to noise in our sample. We show the results for the fits in Figures fig:UGC7321-velocitydispersion-thin and fig:UGC7321-velocitydispersion-thick . The rotation curve rises steeply in the inner parts, only to level of around 9 kpc at 110 km/s. A similar behavior was reported by Uson2003A and OBrien2010A .

The high quality of the data has also allowed us to fit the velocity dispersion. The optically thin model hovers between 9 and 10 km/s up to 10 kpc radius. The self-absorption model yields a decrease from 10 to 8 km/s in the first 3 kpc, but remains flat at 8 km/s up to 10 kpc radius. The optically thin model has a total mass of 8.9 0.4 10^8 M _ , while the self-absorption model has a total mass of 1.17 0.02 10^9 M _ . A total of 24 1 of the is thus hidden by self-absorption.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 as a superthin, bulgeless low-surface-brightness disk whose vertical thickness favors a massive dark halo, with more than two thirds of its overall mass in the halo.

quotes.quote: As an example, we consider the modeling of UGC 7321. The galaxy UGC 7321 reveals itself as a superthin and bulgeless disk galaxy. The disk thickness was derived by . We choose the systematic radial velocity of 394 km/s as that the maximum rotational velocity at the periphery has the similar values ( V 100 km/s) in both sides of the galaxy. The curves 5 and 6 in Fig.14 are calculated for the models with =2.3 and 1.5 , respectively, inside of =R_ max .
As it is seen, the model with > 3 gives a better agreement with the observed radial distribution of the vertical thickness. The best fit model with =2.3 has M_d=0.62 10^ 10 M_ and M_h=1.4 10^ 10 M_ .
As an exception, the superthin LSB galaxy UGC 7321 own the dark halo which contains more than 2/3 of overall galaxy mass.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC7321 as an edge-on disk galaxy, finding strong HI flaring constraints and a spherical dark halo density distribution dominated by dark matter.

quotes.quote: In this paper we analyse these data for the edge-on galaxy UGC7321. We measured the stellar mass distribution ( M=3 10^8 with M/L_R 0.2 ), finding that the vertical force of the gas disk dominates the stellar disk at all radii. Measurements of both the rotation curve and the vertical force field showed that the vertical force puts a much stronger constraint on the stellar mass-to-light ratio than rotation curve decomposition.

Fitting of the vertical force field derived from the flaring of the \ layer and \ velocity dispersion revealed that UGC7321 has a spherical halo density distribution with a flattening of q = /a = 1.0 0.1 . A pseudo-isothermal halo and a gaseous disk with a declining \ velocity dispersion at high latitudes may also fit the vertical force field of UGC7321, but to date there is no observational evidence that the \ velocity dispersion declines away from the galactic plane.

These authors also conclude from their work that the dark matter halo dominates the dynamics of UGC 7321 at all radii, but they do not put constraints on the dark matter halo flattening. Our analysis of UGC7321 has shown that the vertical gradient of the vertical force provides a significantly stronger constraint on the halo density distribution than does rotation curve decomposition.

sources.summary: The paper targets UGC 7321 as an isolated edge-on late-type galaxy, models its H I outskirts, and derives especially stringent non-detection limits on UVB-powered H emission.

quotes.quote: Despite the sensitivity of the VIRUS-P spectrograph on the McDonald 2.7m telescope to low surface brightness emission and the large field-of-view, we do not detect H to 5 upper limits of 6.4 10^ 19 erg/s/cm ^2 / \ in UGC 7321 and of 25 10^ 19 erg/s/cm ^2 / \ in UGC 1281 in each of the hundreds of independent spatial elements (fibers). We fit gas distribution models from overlapping 21 cm data of HI, extrapolate one scale length beyond the HI data, and estimate predicted H surface brightness maps. We analyze three types of limits from the data with stacks formed from increasingly large spatial regions and compare to the model predictions: 1) single fibers, 2) convolution of the fiber grid with a Gaussian, circular kernel (10 \ full width half maximum), and 3) the coadded spectra from a few hundred fibers over the brightest model regions. None of these methods produce a significant detection ( >5 ) with the most stringent constraints on the HI photoionization rate of (z=0)<1.7 10^ 14 s ^ 1 in UGC 7321 and (z=0)<14 10^ 14 s ^ 1 in UGC 1281.

The UGC 7321 limit is below previous measurement limits and also below current theoretical models. Restricting the analysis to the fibers bound by the HI data leads to a comparable limit; the limit is (z=0)<2.3 10^ 14 s ^ 1 in UGC 7321.

Indeeed, our most constraining target, UGC 7321, has a gas surface density below that required for significant star formation Ken89 at all radii, as well as being unusually isolated with no known companions and minimal ( <3 ) warping Uso03 . In this paper we begin with a description of the simple ionization state and density model of disk galaxies that will be used to link a measured H surface brightness with a particular UVB photoiozation rate in sec_mod_HI . In sec_mod_fit , we give disk parameter constraints based on fits to existing 21 cm data. In sec_alt_lim , we argue that UGC 7321 in particular is likely to extend its HI profile beyond the current 21 cm limits without truncation. In addition, the HI observations of UGC 7321 are amongst the most sensitive such measurements published to-date.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 as an edge-on low-surface-brightness galaxy, using observed constraints to explain its stellar and HI disk flaring inside a compact dark matter halo.

quotes.quote: We theoretically study the vertical structure of the edge-on low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy UGC 7321. This is one of the few well-observed LSBs. We modeled it as a gravitationally coupled disk system of stars and atomic hydrogen gas in the potential of the dark matter halo and treated the realistic case where the rotation velocity varies with radius. We used a dense and compact halo as implied by the observed rotation curve in this model.

We chose UGC 7321 for this detailed study because it is one of the few LSB galaxies whose vertical structure has been observationally studied. UGC 7321 was first studied in detail by Matthews et al. (1999) in optical and near-infrared imaging. Matthews (2000) analyzed the H- and R-band data and the B-R color maps to probe its vertical structure, and thus measured the vertical scale height of the stellar disk at different galactocentric radii and noted a radially increasing scale height, but did not explore this further theoretically.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC7321 as a prototypical low-surface-brightness superthin galaxy whose dark-matter-dominated disk, scale heights, and rotation curve can be fitted with a braneworld Weyl-fluid dark mass model.

quotes.quote: Towards this end, we consider the prototypical low surface brightness superthin galaxy, UGC7321, which was found to be dark matter dominated at all radii banerjee2010dark . In fact, have shown that the superthin vertical structure of the stellar disc crucially depends on its dense and compact dark matter halo. Therefore, in this work, we intend to constrain its dark matter density profile in the brane world scenario using the observed stellar and HI scale heights of UGC 7321.

Input Parameters UGC 7321 is a prototypical superthin galaxy with a radial-to-vertical axis ratio of 10.3. It is observed at an inclination of 88^ and is at a distance of 10 Mpc matthews2000h,matthews1999extraordinary . The galaxy has a steeply rising rotation curve with an asymptotic velocity of about 110 km s ^ 1 uson2003hi .

Our analysis reveals that the Weyl model can not only address the observations related to the rotation curve but can also successfully explain the vertical scaleheight data of UGC 7321 within the error bars.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7321 as a nearby edge-on LSB galaxy whose central disk contains clumped dust and strong radial color gradients not explained by dust alone.

quotes.quote: Matthews et al. (1999) have shown that UGC 7321 is an example of a nearby ( D 10 Mpc) LSB galaxy viewed near edge-on ( i=88^ ). UGC 7321 has L_ B =1.0 10^ 9  L_ , M_ HI /L_ B =1.1 ( M_ /L_ ), and a deprojected central surface brightness _ B,i (0) 23.6  mag arcsec ^ 2 . A comprehensive description of the global properties of this galaxy can be found in Matthews et al. (1999).

Fig. 1 shows an 2.6 kpc-long portion of the central disk region of UGC 7321 as imaged by the WFPC2 on HST . It is clear from this image that although UGC 7321 lacks a true dust lane, it is by no means dust-free. Moreover, the dust is seen to exhibit a clumped structure, with the size of the individual clumps ( 30-60 pc) reminiscent of Giant Molecular Clouds in the Milky Way. An intriguing property of UGC 7321 noted by Matthews et al. (1999) is that this galaxy exhibits strong radial B-R color gradients: (B-R) 1.0 from the disk center to its outermost observed edge.

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 7321 as a well-studied superthin galaxy, noting its compact dark halo, underevolved stellar disk, old stars, ongoing heating, and possible weak hidden bar.

quotes.quote: It is worth mentioning that UGC 7321 is an well-studied case of superthin galaxies Matthewsetal1999,Matthews2000 . Their study suggests that the superthin galaxy UGC 7321 is an underevolved system both in dynamical and star forming sense. From the disc colors and color gradients, they also indicate that UGC 7321 is not a young galaxy. There is a population of old disc stars above the disc midplane indicating some ongoing dynamical heating in the galaxy.

In fact, the position velocity diagram in HI line Uson2003 and the deviation of the light profiles in the inner region, the shape of the isophotes in R-band Pohlenetal2003 -all together indicate that there might be a thin bar hidden in the galaxy, UGC 7321. Based on our present analysis, we can comment that such a thin bar (if present in UGC 7321) must be weak; otherwise it would have gone through a bar buckling instability transforming the superthin disc to a thin one.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 as a high-signal galaxy whose rotation curve and vertical force gradient favor a strongly prolate dark matter halo.

quotes.quote: UGC 7321 has the highest signal to noise ratio for the data from our sample. The galaxy was previously modelled by OBrien2010D , who found that the halo flattening q was round ( q=1.0 0.1 ). Their modelling strategy consisted of a two-pass scheme, in which they first performed a rotation curve decomposition, and only then performed a separate fit to the vertical force gradient. This second fit however failed, and the authors had to drastically deviate from the results from the rotation curve decomposition, and use a very low mass stellar disc, in order to reproduce the observed vertical force gradient.

Both the rotation curve and the vertical force gradient are well reproduced. The halo is very prolate, q =1.90_ 0.32 ^ +0.10 and has a high central density of _ 0, halo = 0.324_ 0.043 ^ +0.067 M _ /pc ^3 and short scale-length of R_c = 0.64_ 0.02 ^ +0.05 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper models UGC 7321 as a superthin LSB galaxy and finds its disk-alone and disk-plus-halo cases do not support discrete global spiral modes.

quotes.quote: First, we study a superthin, LSB galaxy UGC 7321 and show that it does not support discrete global spiral modes when modeled as a disk-alone system or as a disk plus dark matter system. Even a moderate increase in the stellar central surface density does not yield any global spiral modes. This naturally explains the observed lack of strong large-scale spiral structure in LSBs.

In our analysis, we have used the input parameters of the LSB galaxy UGC 7321, which is an edge-on galaxy (angle of inclination 88 ^ , MA00 ). However, we note that the generic features of typical LSBs that play the dominant role in the dynamics of disks in LSBs, i.e., low surface density and dark matter dominance from the innermost radius, are both observed in UGC 7321. Due to the edge on nature of this galaxy it is difficult to conclude if large scale spiral features are absent in this galaxy, although the absence of dust lanes is suggestive of this fact.

sources.summary: The paper finds UGC 7321 has the best combined fit in the sample, scale dimensions consistent with prior work, and evidence consistent with recent star formation.

quotes.quote: UGC 7321 UGC 7321 has been modelled using five bands (Figure fig:stellar-UGC7321 ), the results of which have the lowest combined ^2 error in this sample. The scale length has been measured at h_ R ,* =2498 349 pc and the scale height at h_ z ,* =187 80 pc. This is in agreement with OBrien2010D , who applied the deprojection method and reported h_ d ,* =2650 pc and h_ z ,* =245 pc. The scale-height was reported as 140-150 pc by Matthews1999A , who did not discern a bulge.

This implies a recent burst of star formation in the galaxy, which matches the detection of a significant fraction of young stars in the disc Matthews1999A . also performed a fit to the vertical hydrostatics of the disc and found a good fit at M_*/L_R of 0.2.

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 7321 as a prototype nearby superthin with steep rotation, low B-band surface brightness, dark-matter dominance, and modeling that confirms an ultra-cold stellar disc.

quotes.quote: UGC7321 UGC 7321 is a protypical nearby superthin galaxy at a distance D=10 Mpc matthews2000extraordinary , inclination i=88^ matthews1999extraordinary and major-to-minor axes ratio b/a=10.3 . Its characterized by a steeply rising rotation curve with an asymptotic velocity 110 kms ^ 1 uson2003hi . The deprojected central surface brightness in B-band is 23.5 mag arcsec ^ 2 matthews1999extraordinary .

We describe the results obtained from dynamical modeling of UGC 7321 as constrained by stellar photometry in the B band in addition to HI radio-synthesis observations. The central stellar dispersion _ 0s = (10.2 0.6) kms ^ 1 , which falls off exponentially with a scalelength of (2.6 0.6) R_ D . In comparison, the central value of the vertical velocity dispersion of the stellar disc in the Milky Way and the Andromeda or M31 tamm2007visible is about 53 kms ^ 1 . This is assuming that the stellar radial velocity dispersion falls off exponentially with a scale length of 2 Rd as is observed in the Galaxy, and also the ratio of the vertical to the radial stellar velocity dispersion is 0.5 at all radii, equal to its observed value in the solar neighbourhood binney2008princeton . This confirms that UGC7321 has an ultra-cold stellar disc with unusually low values of the vertical velocity dispersion of stars.

sources.summary: The paper compares UGC 7321 with IC 5249, describing UGC 7321 as a genuinely superthin, small galaxy with very small scaleheight and lower stellar vertical velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: Especially the thorough study of UGC 7321 by Matthews and co-workers is interesting in that it gives values for the ratio of the scale parameters from 14 in the inner regions to 10 further out. It also provides the smallest measured value for a scaleheight (140-150 pc) in a stellar disk. UGC 7321 appears to be a superthin galaxy in the real sense.

In fact UGC 7321 is indeed very thin is absolute measure, while IC 5249 should in that respect be seen as relatively thick. However, UGC 7321 is a small galaxy in the sense of the linear size, while IC 5249 is not. Matthews (2000), using quite similar arguments as here, estimates that at one scalelength the vertical velocity dispersion of the stars is only of the order of 12 km/s or so.

sources.summary: The review describes UGC 7321 as a super-thin low-surface-brightness disk with a small scaleheight and low disk mass-to-light ratio, far below maximum disk.

quotes.quote: A prime example is the galaxy UGC 7321, studied extensively by mgvd99 , mat00 , mw03 , um03 and bmj10 . This is a very low surface brightness galaxy (its face-on B band central surface brightness would be 23.4 mag arcsec ^ 2 ) with a scalelength of about 2 kpc, but a projected vertical scaleheight of only 150 pc.

The first galaxy for which this analysis has been completed is UGC 7321 obfvdk10d . The halo density distribution was modelled as a pseudo-isothermal spheroid srjf94 and the disk as a double exponential determined from R band surface photometry. For UGC 7321, the M/L in the disk is very low: the contribution of the stellar disk to the radial force is small and is far below `maximum disk' in this low surface brightness galaxy reached the same conclusion bmj10 .

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 7321 as a low-surface-brightness edge-on Sd galaxy whose H-emission non-detection gave a stringent photoionization-rate limit, but questions that limit after a later claim of H detection.

quotes.quote: Adams \ (2011) used integral-field spectroscopy to set deep limits on H emission from the outskirts of two low surface brightness, edge-on Sd galaxies, UGC 7321 and UGC 1281. They constrained the \ photoionization rate to be _ H < 2.3 10^ 14  s ^ 1 (UGC 7321) and _ H < 14 10^ 14  s ^ 1 (UGC 1281). The UGC 7321 limit comes from a ( 5 ) absence of H , and it lies well below previous observational limits and theoretical predictions.

However, we are reluctant to adopt such a low value of _ H or _0 from a single object Their stringent limit comes from UGC 7321, whereas the limit toward UGC 1281 is a factor of 6 higher. In a subsequent AAS abstract (Uson \ 2012) the same authors claim to have detected H emission toward UGC 7321, with an inferred _H = 3.4 10^ 14  s ^ 1 that is larger than their 2011 upper limit.

sources.summary: The paper reports extended H emission at the tip of UGC 7321's disk, interpreting the fading surface brightness and HI maps as UV-background fluorescence from an ionization front.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of extended H emission from the tip of the \ disk of the nearby edge-on galaxy UGC 7321, observed with the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) instrument at the Very Large Telescope. The H surface brightness fades rapidly where the \ column density drops below N_ HI 10^ 19 cm ^ 2 , consistent with fluorescence arising at the ionisation front from gas that is photoionized by the extragalactic ultraviolet background (UVB). The surface brightness measured at this location is (1.2 0.5) 10^ 19 , where the error is mostly systematic and results from the proximity of the signal to the edge of the MUSE field of view, and from the presence of a sky line next to the redshifted H wavelength.

By combining the H and the \ 21 cm maps with a radiative transfer calculation of an exponential disk illuminated by the UVB, we derive a value for the \ photoionization rate of _ HI (6-8) 10^ 14 s^ 1 . This value is consistent with transmission statistics of the Ly forest and with recent models of a UVB which is dominated by quasars.

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 7321 as an isolated low-mass edge-on galaxy whose thin and thick disk truncations align with a star-formation threshold and internal formation mechanisms.

quotes.quote: Our photometric analysis of the edge-on galaxy UGC 7321 offers a possible breakthrough. This well-studied diffuse, isolated, bulgeless, ultra-thin galaxy is thought to be under-evolved both dynamically and in SF. It is an ideal target for disentangling internal effects in the formation of thick disks and truncations.
We thus show that thick disks and truncations can form via internal mechanisms alone, given the pristine nature of UGC 7321.
UGC 7321 is catalogued as an isolated galaxy by 2005A&A...436..443V and 2017A&A...603A..18H .

sources.summary: The paper uses MUSE observations and radiative-transfer models of UGC 7321's gaseous disk to interpret diffuse outer H emission as UV-background fluorescence and refine the local photoionization rate.

quotes.quote: In a recent paper fumagalli17 we reported on the detection of a diffuse H glow in the outskirts of the nearby, edge-on disc galaxy UGC 7321 observed with the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) at the ESO Very Large Telescope. By interpreting the H emission as fluorescence arising from hydrogen ionised by an external (i.e., extragalactic) radiation field, we estimated the UV background (UVB) intensity in terms of ionisation rate (per ion) at z 0 to be in the range _ 6-8 10^ 14 s ^ 1 . In the present work, by performing radiative transfer calculations over a large set of models of the gaseous disc of UGC 7321, we refine our estimate and through an MCMC analysis derive a value for the photoionisaton rate of _ =7.27^ +2.93 _ 2.90 10^ 14 s ^ 1 . In particular, our analysis demonstrates that this value is robust against large variations in the galaxy model and that the uncertainties are mainly driven by the errors associated with the observed \ surface brightness.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 07321 as an Additional-sample edge-on galaxy with excess FIR emission at large vertical distances, interpreting the extraplanar dust signal as real rather than PSF or inclination driven.

quotes.quote: Six galaxies from the Additional sample (IC 2233, NGC 3592, UGC 07321, UGC 07387, UGC 07522, and UGC 09242) also show an excess of FIR emission compared to our models at large vertical distances. We note that this extraplanar FIR emission, which typically starts to dominate at heights 1--2 kpc above the mid-plane, cannot be explained by the scattered light of the PSF since we accounted for this effect while modelling. Also, variations in the galaxy inclination angle as described in Sect. sec:method cannot explain the observed excess of dust emission (the red spread along the model profile in Fig. fig:profiles shows a one- spread of the model profiles for varied inclination angles, as described in Sect. sec:3Ddisc ). This indicates that the observed extraplanar emission is real and produced by the dust at high distances above the mid-plane.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 7321 as a nearby isolated superthin LSB edge-on galaxy with peanut-shaped outer isophotes, interpreted with H I evidence as an early bar-buckling case before a true bulge formed.

quotes.quote: The low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy UGC 7321, a nearby, isolated, ``superthin'' edge-on galaxy, is an ideal object to study those purely disk dominated bulge-less galaxies. Although late type spirals are believed to exhibit the simplest possible structure, even prior observations showed deviations from a pure single component exponential disk in the case of UGC 7321. We present for the first time photometric evidence for peanut-shaped outer isophotes from a deep optical (R-band) image of UGC 7321. Observations and dynamical modeling suggest that boxy/peanut-shaped (b/p) bulges in general form through the buckling instability in bars of the parent galaxy disks. Together with recent HI observations supporting the presence of a stellar bar in UGC 7321 this could be the earliest known case of the buckling process during the evolutionary life of a LSB galaxy, whereby material in the disk-bar has started to be pumped up above the disk, but a genuine bulge has not yet formed.
